1:
Ps 28 superscription: 'A Psalm of David.' The psalm is a personal lament transitioning to communal thanksgiving in vv6-9. The shift from individual petition (vv1-5) to communal blessing (vv8-9) suggests liturgical use at the Jerusalem sanctuary. David functions as representative of the whole people before YHWH.

'My Rock' (tzuri) — the epithet sur (rock/crag) is the Mosaic appellative for YHWH developed in Deut 32 (the Song of Moses): 'The Rock — his work is perfect' (Deut 32:4). The rock imagery evokes the Sinai landscape and Mosaic covenant tradition. 'If you are silent I become like those who go down to the pit' — the fear is not merely death but divine abandonment, which in ANE thought meant losing access to the deity's protective power.
2: 'Lifting hands toward your Most Holy Place' (el-devir qodshecha) — devir is the inner sanctuary, the Holy of Holies, where the ark of the covenant resided. Prayer directed toward the devir represents the belief that YHWH's presence was specially localized there — a theology developed in 1 Kgs 8 (Solomon's temple dedication prayer). Before the temple was built, prayer may have been directed toward the Tent of Meeting. The gesture of lifted hands (nisheat chapai) is attested in Mesopotamian and Egyptian prayer postures: arms raised, palms up, in supplication before the deity. Ps 141:2 also connects lifted hands to incense offering — the bodily posture is a form of sacrifice.
3: 'Who speak peace to their neighbors while plotting evil in their hearts' — the social context is the royal court where political alliances were sealed with covenantal language of shalom (peace/wholeness) while treachery operated beneath. The ANE treaty tradition used shalom vocabulary to ratify suzerain-vassal relationships; speaking peace falsely was therefore a covenant violation. This pattern is reflected in Absalom's conspiracy (2 Sam 15) and the political intrigues surrounding David. The legal category of false witness (ed shaqer) was a specific covenant-court crime.
4: 'Give them according to their work' — the lex talionis principle (retributive justice in exact proportion) underpins the petition. In the ANE, divine retribution was understood as the deity enforcing the cosmic moral order. The plea that the wicked experience what they intended for others is rooted in Deuteronomic covenant theology: blessings for obedience, curses for betrayal (Deut 27-28). The petition is not mere vengeance but an appeal for YHWH to be the cosmic enforcer of covenant justice.
5: 'Because they do not understand the works of the LORD' — the theological diagnosis of wickedness is moral blindness to divine governance. In wisdom tradition (Prov 1:7; Ps 14:1), failure to acknowledge YHWH is the root of ethical disorder. The works of YHWH (maasot YHWH) in Psalmic theology are both the works of creation (Ps 8) and the works of history (Ps 77:11; 78:7). To be blind to these is to be structurally disoriented. The consequence — 'he will tear them down and not build them up' — echoes Jeremiah's call to 'tear down and build up' (Jer 1:10): destruction is the divine response to covenant breach.
6: 'Blessed be the LORD, for he has heard the voice of my pleas' — the pivot of the psalm: a sudden shift to praise in the perfect tense. This 'praise in advance' or 'certainty of hearing' is a characteristic form of Hebrew lament psalmody — the petitioner becomes so confident of divine response that the tone shifts from petition to thanksgiving before the answer arrives. This reflects the liturgical structure of the sanctuary: lament was offered and then priestly assurance of hearing may have been given (an Heilsorakel, salvation oracle), which the worshiper received as the answer.
7: 'My heart trusts in him and I am helped' — batach (trust/rely on) is the covenant-confidence vocabulary, contrasted with trusting in human military power (Ps 20:7; Isa 31:1). The movement from petition to confidence to praise follows the standard lament-psalm trajectory. 'My heart leaps for joy' — the physical location of emotion in the lev (heart/mind): ANE anthropology located reasoning, emotion, and will all in the heart-organ.
8: 'The LORD is the strength of his people; he is the saving refuge of his anointed' — mashiach (his anointed) here likely refers to the Davidic king as YHWH's covenant representative. The theology of the anointed king as the conduit of divine strength for the nation is developed in the Davidic covenant texts (2 Sam 7; Ps 2; 89). The king is ma'oz (strong fortress) for the people not through personal power but as the channel of YHWH's protection.
9: 'Save your people and bless your inheritance; shepherd them and carry them forever' — the psalm closes with communal prayer on behalf of all Israel, YHWH's nachalah (inheritance/estate). Israel as YHWH's inheritance is Deuteronomic theology (Deut 4:20; 9:29): the nation is YHWH's personal property, as land is a family's inheritance. 'Shepherd them' — the royal shepherd metaphor: kings in the ANE were regularly called shepherds of their people (Hammurabi's law code prologue; the Mesopotamian royal ideal). YHWH as shepherd of Israel reframes the royal function as divine rather than human.
